text: Five trocars, including four robotic trocars, are inserted into the abdominal cavity, followed by an episcopic evaluation of the surgical field.
The surgeon initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) using a transdental approach, addressing the anticipated technical challenges of robotic or laparoscopic methods.
The surgeon initiates interstitial resection at the level of the dentate line using a Lone Star retractor, aiming to achieve negative resection margins.

The surgeon performs an intrastringent resection of the internal anal sphincter, extending approximately two centimeters. The TEO endoscopic platform is transiently inserted, and the rectum is insufflated with CO₂.
The surgeon performs an additional intrastringent resection endoscopically using a bipolar device, focusing on precise tissue removal within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the third dissection using the viperspring technique, revealing the anatomical plane between the rectum and the prostate anteriorly, and reaching the presacral space posteriorly.
The surgeon occludes the rectum endoscopically using a viperspring suture with the endostitch device, securing the closure within the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum and mesorectum using sharp dissection techniques, adhering to the principles of total mesorectal excision after achieving complete occlusion of the inner rectal stump.
The surgeon carefully dissects the plane between the anterior rectal wall and the posterior prostate using cautery and a bipolar device, ensuring precise separation of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally, maintaining the established plane between the rectum and surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects while preserving the integrity of the mesorectum and protecting the lateral pelvic-side wall nerves, ensuring these structures remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon completes circumferential mobilization of the rectum and mesorectum using sharp dissection techniques, progressing cephalad towards the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon carefully dissects anteriorly, ensuring the preservation of the prostate and seminal vesicles during the procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection along the presacral plane, carefully separating the posterior rectal wall from the sacrum using surgical instruments.
The surgeon reaches and divides the peritoneal reflection, confirming entry into the peritoneal cavity from below, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon completes circumferential dissection of the mesorectum and rectum, but progress is temporarily limited due to poor exposure and insufficient traction from above.
The robotic arms are docked into position, and the surgeon performs the abdominal portion of the total mesorectal excision (TME) using robotic assistance.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to elevate the sigmoid colon, placing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) under tension. The IMA is scored with cautery and carefully dissected from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) after it has been fully mobilized.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to dissect the remaining sigmoid mesentery, completing the medial to lateral mobilization of the tissue.
The surgeon initiates pelvic dissection using a transdental endoscopic approach to complete the mobilization of residual rectal and mesorectal attachments, which were not previously divided.
The surgeon reaches the previous plane of dissection from the transdental approach using a robotic abdominal technique, mobilizing the residual attachments and completely freeing the rectum.
The surgeon removes the robotic arms and completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure using a laparoscopic approach, manipulating the colon to achieve the necessary exposure.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) using robotic instruments, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the proximal colon, completing the mobilization of this section.
After completing the laparoscopic takedown of the plantar fascia, the specimen is transiently exteriorized with laparoscopic assistance, showing the specimen outside the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon carefully monitors the transient extraction of the colon, ensuring there are no twists in the colon or its mesentery during the procedure.
The surgeon transects the specimen transanally and performs a hand-sewn colloidal anastomosis, completing the connection between the remaining bowel segments.

The surgeon creates a laparoscopic loop ileostomy, positioning the loop of ileum through the abdominal wall. A pelvic drain is visibly placed in the pelvic cavity to manage postoperative fluid accumulation.
